title: The body acceptance by others scale-2: an examination of its psychometric properties in a French-Canadian adult sample
abstract: The 13-item Body Acceptance by Others Scale-2 (BAOS-2) is an instrument designed to measure generalised perceptions of body acceptance by others. Here, we examined the psychometric properties of a novel French translation of the BAOS-2. A total of 763 French-speaking adults from Canada were asked to complete the BAOS-2, alongside additional validated measures of positive body image, psychological well-being, and eating patterns. Exploratory factor analysis in a first split-half subsample (n = 382) indicated that a unidimensional model with all 13 items had good fit to the data. This model was cross-validated in a second split-half subsample (n = 381) using confirmatory factor analysis. A partial factors-only model indicated, as a function of subsamples, that the French BAOS-2 has differential item functioning based on respondent age, body mass index (BMI), and gender identity (men, women). In addition, higher respondent BMI was associated with lower body acceptance in both subsamples. Our results also supported convergent and concurrent validity of the French BAOS-2, with body acceptance by others associated with greater body appreciation and psychological well-being, as well as lower symptoms of maladaptive eating patterns. The present results indicate that the French BAOS-2 has strong psychometric properties in French-speaking Canadian adults.

The Body Acceptance by Others Scale-2: An examination of its 
psychometric properties in a French-Canadian adult sample1

Body acceptance by others

A B S T R A C T
The 13-item Body Acceptance by Others Scale-2 (BAOS-2) is an instrument designed to measure generalised 
perceptions of body acceptance by others. Here, we examined the psychometric properties of a novel French 
translation of the BAOS-2. A total of 763 French-speaking adults from Canada were asked to complete the BAOS- 
2, alongside additional validated measures of positive body image, psychological well-being, and eating patterns. 
Exploratory factor analysis in a first split-half subsample (n = 382) indicated that a unidimensional model with 
all 13 items had good fit to the data. This model was cross-validated in a second split-half subsample (n = 381) 
using confirmatory factor analysis. A partial factors-only model indicated, as a function of subsamples, that the 
French BAOS-2 has differential item functioning based on respondent age, body mass index (BMI), and gender 
identity (men, women). In addition, higher respondent BMI was associated with lower body acceptance in both 
subsamples. Our results also supported convergent and concurrent validity of the French BAOS-2, with body 
acceptance by others associated with greater body appreciation and psychological well-being, as well as lower 
symptoms of maladaptive eating patterns. The present results indicate that the French BAOS-2 has strong psy­
chometric properties in French-speaking Canadian adults.
1. Introduction
Body acceptance by others refers to “a sense that one’s body and its 
physical characteristics are valued, respected, and unconditionally 
accepted by important others… irrespective of what others or social 
domains actually offer at any particular time” (Swami, Todd, Stieger 
et al., 2021, p. 239). Defined in this way and consistent with the 
Acceptance Model of Intuitive Eating (Avalos & Tylka, 2006), body 
acceptance by others is not itself a facet of body image, but is instead 
theorised as an antecedent of facets of positive body image. More spe­
cifically, when individuals have access to external relational connec­
tions and social systems that are validating, they are likely to feel 
empowered to resist self-objectification, disengage from restrictive 
appearance-related pressures, and adopt adaptive body-related attitudes 
that result in more positive body image (Avalos & Tylka, 2006; Tigge­
mann, 2019). Indeed, there is a large body of evidence showing that 
greater body acceptance by others is significantly associated with more 
positive body image (e.g., Chan et al., 2023; Longhurst & Swami, 2023; 
Swami et al., 2018, 2023, 2025) and embodied well-being (Moelle & 
Papps, 2025) in diverse communities.
The construct of body acceptance by others was originally measured 
using the Body Acceptance by Others Scale (BAOS; Avalos & Tylka, 
2006). This is a 10-item instrument in which respondents are asked to 
rate the perceived acceptance of their body shape and weight, and the 
degree to which they receive messages that their body shape and weight 
are “fine”, from five sources (i.e., friends, family, dating partners, mass 
media, and society). In their initial assessment, Avalos and Tylka (2006)
reported that scores on the BAOS had adequate composite reliability,

test-retest reliability up to three weeks, and construct validity. However, 
more recent work has reported difficulties replicating a unidimensional 
model of BAOS scores (Swami, Furnham, Horne, & Stieger, 2020; 
Swami, Todd et al., 2020). Additional concerns with the BAOS include 
its specific focus on body weight and shape rather than body and its 
physical characteristics more holistically (i.e., what is being measured), 
the limiting of body acceptance by others to the receipt of messages as 
opposed to perceived body acceptance (i.e., how the construct is 
defined), and the focus on specific targets rather than generalised per­
ceptions of body acceptance (i.e., how the construct is measured). Such 
concerns led to the development of a revised version of the BAOS.
The second iteration of the BAOS – the BAOS-2 (Swami, Todd, 
Stieger et al., 2021) – is a 13-item instrument that assesses generalised 
perceptions of body acceptance by others. In adults from the United 
Kingdom, Swami, Todd, Stieger et al. (2021) reported that scores on the 
BAOS-2 had a unidimensional factor structure, with adequate composite 
reliability, test-retest reliability up to four weeks, and good convergent, 
construct, criterion, discriminant, and incremental validity. Addition­
ally, these authors also reported that the BAOS-2 achieved strong 
invariance across gender identity and that there was no significant dif­
ference in body acceptance by others between women and men. Finally, 
Swami, Todd, Stieger et al. (2021) also reported that scores on the 
BAOS-2 achieved strong invariance across adults from the United 
Kingdom and the United States. Although participants in the United 
Kingdom were found to have significantly higher (manifest) BAOS-2 
scores compared to their counterparts from the United States, the ef­
fect size of the difference was negligible.
A small number of studies have examined the psychometric prop­
erties of translated versions of the BAOS-2. In terms of factorial validity, 
studies using a combination of exploratory factor analysis (EFA) and 
confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) have reported that the BAOS-2 re­
tains its unidimensional factor structure in adults from Malaysia (Swami 
et al., 2021), China (Wang et al., 2022), Germany (Behrend et al., 2023), 
Poland (Modrzejewska et al., 2024), and Italy (Nerini et al., 2024). One 
study using only EFA has also reported that the unidimensional model of 
the BAOS-2 is supported in Israeli women (Geller et al., 2024). Where it 
has been assessed, authors of these translations have also reported that 
the unidimensional model is supported in women and men separately, 
and that scores achieve strong invariance across gender identity. Addi­
tionally, comparisons of scores across women and men have generally 
indicated non-significant or negligible gender identity differences in all 
cases.
In addition, there is some evidence that the BAOS-2 is invariant 
across racialised identities (Malaysian Malays vs. Chinese) and national 
groups (Malaysia, the United Kingdom, and the United States; Swami 
et al., 2021). Beyond factorial validity, test adaptation studies have 
generally reported that the BAOS-2 has adequate convergent validity (e. 
g., positive associations with facets of positive body image) and con­
current validity (e.g., positive associations with psychological 
well-being and negative associations with pressure to internalise 
appearance ideals; Modrzejewska et al., 2024; Nerini et al., 2024; Swami 
et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Among Chinese adults, Wang et al. 
(2022) additionally reported that baseline BAOS-2 scores were related to 
improvements in body and functionality appreciation six months later, 
indicative of predictive validity. In short, the available evidence sug­
gests that the BAOS-2 retains its robust psychometric properties across 
translational contexts.
1.1. The French-Canadian context
To add to this body of translational work, we sought to examine the 
psychometric properties of a novel French translation of the BAOS-2 in 
Canadian adults. Recently, the BAOS-2 has been translated into French, 
but with items altered to specifically refer to body acceptance by one’s 
father and mother, respectively (Lacerte et al., 2025). An EFA revealed 
that scores reduced to two dimensions, reflective of a factor for each 
parent, in French-speaking adolescent girls from Canada. However, this 
does not necessarily indicate true instrument unidimensionality (i.e., the 
two dimensions are likely an artefact of item wording) and, to our 
knowledge, the psychometric properties of the French BAOS-2 in its 
original form has not been assessed. While adding to the range of lan­
guages in which the psychometric properties of the BAOS-2 remains an 
important task (Swami, Todd, Stieger et al., 2021), assessing the in­
strument’s psychometric properties in French Canadian adults may be 
interesting because of the uniqueness of this population.
First, the Francophone population remains a minority social identity 
in the Canadian federation (Wu & Wilkes, 2017). This minority status 
has been suggested to be a reason for higher levels of social connect­
edness and group-based self-conceptualisations among Francophone 
Canadians compared to Anglophone Canadians (e.g., Clift, 1989; 
Latouche, 
2006). 
Additionally, 
French 
Canadian 
(particularly 
Qu´eb´ecois) roots in Catholicism and a rejection of eighteenth-century 
liberal individualism (e.g., Dufort et al., 2022; Richer & Laporte, 
1973) may provide additional pressures toward collectivist trends. Such 
influences may heighten sensitivity to social cues and attendance to the 
judgements and emotional responses of others (Swami et al., 2021), 
which in turn may affect the way in which body acceptance by others is 
experienced. Indeed, relatively higher levels of collectivism in French 
Canada may mean that individuals are highly attuned to the perceptions 
of others and that body acceptance by others is integral to the devel­
opment and shaping of one’s self-concept. To be sure, the extent to 
which these historic trends continue to exert an effect on contemporary 
Francophone Canada is a matter of some debate (e.g., Wu & Dawson, 
2022), but nevertheless, Francophone Canada would appear to be an 
interesting region in which to assess body acceptance by others.
The contemporary body image literature also suggests that body 
acceptance by others may be an important factor to consider in Fran­
cophone Canada. For example, one study of Qu´eb´ecois adolescents 
showed that nearly a third had received commentary about their bodies 
or appearance from others, with the majority of commentary originating 
from close others (e.g., peers, siblings, parents; Aim´e & Maïano, 2014). 
A more recent study of Qu´eb´ecois adolescents reported that nearly a half 
had received weight-based commentary, primarily from close friends, 
which in turn was associated with appearance dissatisfaction (Gagn´e 
et al., 2023). However, gaps remain in our understanding of these issues, 
particularly as body acceptance by others is not necessarily the polar 
opposite of appearance-related commentary (Swami, Todd, Stieger 
et al., 2021). In other words, a lack of appearance-related commentary 
by itself cannot be used to understand or estimate high levels of un­
conditional body acceptance from others. A fuller understanding of the 
construct of body acceptance by others and its correlates in the 
French-Canadian context would, therefore, be highly informative.
1.2. The present study
In the present work, our primary objective was to assess the psy­
chometric properties of a novel French translation of the BAOS-2 in a 
sample of French-Canadian adults. In terms of assessing factorial val­
idity, current guidelines recommend the use of and EFA-to-CFA strategy 
when a construct is hypothesised to be unidimensional, as is the case for 
the BAOS-2 (Swami, Todd, Stieger et al., 2021). This strategy combines 
an exploratory approach in which the most suitable model for a dataset 
is extracted, followed by a confirmatory approach in which the 
best-fitting model is cross-validated in a new sample or subsample 
(Swami & Barron, 2019; Swami, Maïano, & Morin, 2023). Additionally, 
we examined invariance of the best factor solution across two inde­
pendent samples and differential item functioning (DIF) of participant 
gender identity (women vs. men), age, and body mass index (BMI) as 
function of the two independent samples. DIF is used as an alternative to 
the more popular multi-group CFA when characteristics occur as 
continuous variables (e.g., age and BMI) or when testing for measure­
ment biases as a joint function of multiple variables (Penfield & Lam,

2000).
Additionally, we examined the construct validity of the French 
BAOS-2. To do so, we examined associations between BAOS-2 scores and 
scores on additional variables that have been validated for use in French- 
speaking populations. More specifically, to assess convergent validity, 
we examined associations with a core facet of positive body image, 
namely body appreciation, with the expectation of a positive and 
moderate-to-strong association. To assess concurrent validity, we 
considered associations with psychological well-being (symptoms of 
anxiety and depression, life satisfaction, and self-esteem), eating pat­
terns (dieting behaviour and orthorexia), and self-compassion. Positive 
and moderate associations between body acceptance by others and life 
satisfaction, self-esteem, self-compassion, and non-pathological (or 
“healthy”) orthorexia were expected. Moreover, we expected negative 
and moderate associations with anxiety, depression, dieting behaviours, 
and orthorexia nervosa.
2. Method
2.1. Participants
The participants of the present study were 763 French-speaking 
Canadian adults aged between 17 and 90 years old (M = 50.87, SD =
15.87). Of this sample, 52.9% identified as women, 45.9% as men, 0.7% 
as non-binary, and 0.5% as another gender. In terms of education, 0.3% 
had completed elementary education, 14.2% had completed high school 
education, 15.5% had completed a diploma of vocational studies, 22.6% 
had completed a pre-university programme (CEGEP), 30.6% had an 
undergraduate degree, and 16.8% had a postgraduate degree. Of the 
total sample, 4.0% and 6.2% reported having currently or having had an 
eating disorder diagnosis, respectively, while 0.7% of women reported 
being pregnant. Finally, participants’ BMI, calculated from self-reported 
height and weight, ranged from 11.48 to 55.35 kg/m² (M = 27.56, SD =
6.44).
2.2. Measures
2.2.1. Participant information
Participants were asked to self-report their age, gender identity, 
highest education degree, height (in foot and inches or meters), and 
weight (in pounds or kilograms). Additionally, participants were asked 
whether: (a) they were currently diagnosed with an eating disorder (0 =
no; 1 = yes); (b) had been diagnosed with an eating disorder in the past 
(0 = no; 1 = yes); and, if they identified as a woman, (c) if they were 
pregnant. Data on eating disorder diagnosis and pregnancy were 
requested for sample descriptive purposes only.
2.2.2. Body acceptance by others
As recommended by Swami and Barron (2019), the BAOS-2 (Swami, 
Todd, Stieger et al., 2021) was translated from English into French as 
follows. First, the BAOS-2 items, instructions, and response scale were 
independently forward-translated by a professional translator and a 
member of the research team. Second, an independent translator (a 
member of the research team) examined these translations, resolved any 
discrepancies, and produced a single, synthesised French version. Third, 
the synthesised French version was back-translated into English by two 
independent professional translators. Finally, the forward- and 
back-translations were inspected by an expert committee comprising all 
previous translators and all members of the research team. Any minor 
discrepancies were resolved by discussion. The final version of the 
BAOS-2 in French are reported in the Supplementary Materials. Re­
sponses to the items were made using a 5-point scale ranging from 1 
(never; French: jamais) to 5 (always; French: toujours).
2.2.3. Body appreciation
Participants completed the Body Appreciation Scale-2 (BAS-2; Tylka 
& Wood-Barcalow, 2015; French translation: Kertechian & Swami, 
2017) to assess a core facet of positive body image. The 10-item BAS-2 
assesses acceptance of one’s body, respect and care for one’s body, 
and protection of one’s body from unrealistic beauty standards. Items 
were rated on a 5-point scale (1 = never, 5 = always) and higher scores 
reflect greater body appreciation. Scores on the BAS-2 have been shown 
to have a unidimensional factor structure, adequate composite reli­
ability, and good construct validity, including in French-speaking Ca­
nadian adults (Swami, Tran et al., 2023). In the present study, 
McDonald’s ω for scores on this scale was .958.
2.2.4. Dieting
Dieting-related attitudes and behaviours were assessed using the 
Dieting subscale of the Eating Attitudes Test-26 (EAT-26; Garner et al., 
1982; French translation: Leichner et al., 1994), which includes 13 items 
that assess the degree of dieting-related attitudes and behaviours char­
acteristic of disordered eating. All items on this subscale were rated on a 
6-point scale ranging from 1 (never) to 6 (always), with higher scores 
reflective of greater dieting attitudes and behaviours. The French 
version of the EAT-26, including the Dieting subscale, has been shown to 
have adequate construct validity and composite reliability in 
French-speaking Canadian adults (Leichner et al., 1994). In the present 
study, McDonald’s ω for scores on this subscale was .926.
2.2.5. Orthorexia
Participants completed the Teruel Orthorexia Scale (TOS; Barrada & 
Roncero, 2018; French translation: Maïano et al., 2022). This is a 
17-item scale that assesses symptoms of orthorexia nervosa (i.e., an 
obsession or extreme preoccupation with eating healthily; 8 items) and 
what the authors refer to as “healthy” orthorexia (i.e., a 
non-pathological form of orthorexia; 9 items). All items were rated on a 
4-point scale ranging from 0 (completely disagree) to 3 (completely agree), 
with higher scores reflective of greater orthorexic symptomatology. The 
French version of the TOS has been shown to have a 2-factor structure 
and adequate psychometric properties in French-speaking Canadian 
adults (Maïano et al., 2022). In the present study, McDonald’s ω was 
.882 for the Orthorexia Nervosa subscale and .874 for the Healthy 
Orthorexia subscale.
2.2.6. Anxiety and depression
Participants completed the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale 
(HADS; Zigmond & Snaith, 1983; French translation: Savard et al., 
1998) to assess anxiety and depression, respectively. The HADS consists 
of 14 items, seven of which assess symptoms of anxiety and seven of 
which assess symptoms of depression. Each item was rated on a 4-point 
scale, with total scores for each subscale ranging from 0 to 21. The 
French version of the HADS has been shown to have good sensitivity to 
determine the presence of anxiety or depressive symptoms, respectively 
(Savard et al., 1998). In the present study, McDonald’s ω was .846 for 
symptoms of anxiety and .819 for symptoms of depression.
2.2.7. Life satisfaction
We asked participants to complete the Satisfaction With Life Scale 
(SWLS; Diener et al., 1985; French translation: Blais et al., 1989), a 
5-item instrument that assesses an aspect of subjective well-being on a 
7-point scale (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree). Higher scores on 
this scale reflect greater life satisfaction. Scores on the French trans­
lation of the SWLS have been shown to have a unidimensional factor 
structure in French-speaking Canadian adults (Swami et al., 2025). In 
the present study, McDonald’s ω for scores on this scale was .918.
2.2.8. Self-compassion
To measure self-compassion, we used the Short Form of the Self- 
Compassion Scale (SCS-SF; Raes et al., 2011; French translation: 
Swami et al., 2025), a 11-item scale that assesses multidimensional 
facets of self-compassion. All items were rated on a 5-point scale,

suggested that the SCS-SF is best conceptualised as consisting of two

2.2.9. Self-esteem
To assess self-esteem, we used the Single-Item Self-Esteem Scale 
(SISE; Robins et al., 2001; French translation: Swami et al., 2020). 
Participants were asked to rate the item “I have high self-esteem” on a 
7-point scale, ranging from 1 (not very true of me) to 7 (very true of me), 
with higher scores reflective of higher self-esteem. Scores on the SISE 
have been reported to have adequate construct validity in diverse pop­
ulations (Swami et al., 2020).
2.3. Procedure
The present study was carried out in accordance with the principles 
of the Declaration of Helsinki and authorisation was granted by the 
research ethics committee of the first and last authors’ university 
(approval code: #2025–3656). The majority (95%) of participants was 
recruited in the Canadian province of Quebec via a panel from Leger 
Opinion and received CAD$1.80 as remuneration. The remainder of 
participants (5%) were recruited in the community via social media 
advertising, targeted emails, advertising in a local newspaper, and pri­
vate clinic websites. No compensation was offered for these participants. 
Data collection took place between November 2024 and April 2025. 
Participants provided digital informed consent and were asked to 
complete an anonymous survey hosted on LimeSurvey, in which the 
order of presentation of instruments described above was presented in a 
pre-determined order. All participants took part on a voluntary basis.
2.4. Statistical analyses
To accommodate an EFA-to-CFA strategy, the total dataset was split 
using a computer-generated random seed. The two split-half subsamples 
consisted of 382 and 381 participants, respectively. No significant dif­
ferences were found between the two split-half subsamples in terms of 
age, t(761) = 1.05, p = .292, d = 0.08; BMI, t(748) = 0.89, p = .373, 
d = 0.07; gender identities, χ2(5) = 4.39, p = .495; and educational 
qualifications, χ2(9) = 4.28, p = .892. Therefore, any differences be­
tween the two split-half subsamples were assumed to have been mini­
mised by the random-split procedure.
2.4.1. First split-half subsample
Mplus 8.11’s (Muth´en & Muth´en, 2024) maximum likelihood robust 
(MLR) estimator was used to handle the missing data at the BAOS-2’s 
item level (1.31%-10.73%, M = 4.99%). Three exploratory factor ana­
lyses (EFAs) were performed on the BAOS-2’s items using an oblique 
geomin rotation and an epsilon value of .5 (Marsh et al., 2009, 2014). 
The best EFA solution was retained based on the Horn’s (1965) parallel 
analysis test and the following fit indices (Hu & Bentler, 1999; Marsh 
et al., 2005): the Comparative Fit Index (CFI), Tucker Lewis Index (TLI), 
and root mean square error of approximation (RMSEA). A model was 
considered as having an acceptable or good fit to the data when CFI and 
TLI values were equal to or higher than .90 or .95 and RMSEA values 
were lower than or equal to .08 or .06, respectively (Hu & Bentler, 1999; 
Marsh et al., 2005). Finally, the McDonald’s (1970) ω was used to es­
timate the composite reliability of latent factor(s).
2.4.2. s split-half subsample
The best EFA solution retained from the first split-half subsample was 
examined using Mplus 8.11’s (Muth´en & Muth´en, 2024) MLR estimator 
(missing at BAOS-2’s item level was between 1.57% and 8.40%, M =
4.62%) and CFA. The same fit indices as reported above (first split-half 
subsample) were used to assess model fit.
2.4.3. First and second split-half subsamples
Using Mplus 8.11 (Muth´en & Muth´en, 2024) MLR estimator, we 
assessed invariance of the optimal solution across subsamples using a 
CFA model and with the following sequence (Millsap, 2011): (i) con­
figural invariance; and invariance of (ii) loadings; (iii) intercepts; (iv) 
uniqueness; (v) latent variance; and (vi) latent mean factor. Comparison 
of the models (i.e., the subsequent model is compared to the previous 
one) were based on changes (Δ) in CFI, TLI, and RMSEA. When ΔCFI 
and/or ΔTLI decreases were higher than .01, and/or the increase in 
ΔRMSEA was higher than .015, a sequence was considered as 
non-invariant (Chen, 2007; Cheung & Rensvold, 2002).
A hybrid MIMIC multiple-group model (Maïano et al., 2023; Morin 
et al., 2018) was used to examine presence of DIF and latent mean dif­
ferences as function of participants’ age, BMI, and gender identity, as 
well as the equivalence of these associations across subsamples. This 
method was favoured relative to multi-group invariance testing, as we 
were examining multiple continuous predictors (i.e., age and BMI) that 
should not be arbitrarily categorised (Swami, Maïano et al., 2023). 
These models were built from the most invariant model identified during 
the measurement invariance test as function of subsamples. Hybrid 
MIMIC models were estimated in the following sequence (Morin, 2023; 
Swami, Maïano et al., 2023): (a) null effects model; (b) saturated model; 
and (c) factors only model. To facilitate interpretation, age and BMI 
were standardized prior to the analyses. These models were first 
examined with all associations freely estimated across subsamples, and 
then the most appropriate model retained was compared to a model in 
which all associations were constrained to be equal across subsamples.
First, we considered that there was a meaningful association between 
participants’ age, BMI, and gender identity and the BAOS-2’s item re­
sponses when the increase of ΔCFI and/or ΔTLI was higher or equal to 
.01 and/or the decrease of ΔRMSEA was higher or equal to .015 between 
the saturated and factors only models relative to the null effects model. 
Second, we considered that there was a DIF when an improvement in 
model fit is observed for the saturated model relative to the factor only 
model. Third, we considered that there was a subsamples equality in the 
association between participants’ age, BMI, and gender identity and the 
BAOS-2’s item responses when lack of improvement in model fit was 
observed between the most appropriate hybrid MIMIC model and an 
alternative version of this model in which all associations were con­
strained to be equal across subsamples.
Construct validity of the BAOS-2 was examined using a structural 
equation model (SEM) in which the BAOS-2 latent factor(s) of the best 
factor solution was correlated with the observed scores of measures of 
anxiety and depression, body appreciation, dieting behaviour, orthor­
exia nervosa and healthy orthorexia, life satisfaction, compassionate 
self-responding (self-compassion), and self-esteem. Moreover, observed 
scores were all correlated in this SEM.
3. Results
3.1. First split-half subsample
Fit indices for the three EFA models are presented in Table 1. All 
three factors solution had acceptable-to-good fit to the data (Models 1–1 
to 1–3). Results from the parallel analysis test showed that the data were 
best represented by a single-factor solution (for a visual representation, 
see Figure S1 in the Supplementary Materials). Examination of the 2-fac­
tor solution showed that the first factor comprised 10 of the 13 items and 
that, in the secondary factor, one of the three items (Item # 11 – “I can 
count on important others to accept my body”) provided improper 
standardised factor loading (1.13) and uniqueness (-.071). Finally, ex­
amination of the 3-factor solution revealed that the first factor was split 
into two factors and that the third factor only comprised a single item

uniqueness (-2.40). Therefore, based on the combination of fit indices, 
analyses of the factor solutions, and results of parallel analysis test, the 
unidimensional solution with all 13 items was retained for subsequent 
analyses. Table 2 presents the standardised parameters and the com­
posite reliability coefficient for this model. Loadings were moderate-to- 
high (λ =.573–.863; Mλ =.775) and the composite reliability coefficient 
was excellent (ω =.952).
3.2. Second Split-half subsample
Fit indices of the unidimensional CFA-based model are presented in 
Table 1. As can be seen, model fit was acceptable, loadings’ values were 
moderate-to-high (λ =.572–.876; Mλ =.772; see Table 2), and the 
composite reliability coefficient of the BAOS-2’s latent factor was 
excellent (ω =.951).

subsamples are presented in Table 3. First, results showed a lack of 
significant association between the BAOS-2’s latent factor and age and 
gender identity, respectively. However, the results also showed that BMI 
was significantly and negatively related to the BAOS-2’s latent factor. 
Therefore, participants with higher BMIs tended to present significantly 
lower levels of body acceptance by others relative to participants with 
lower BMIs.
The SEM examining the construct validity of the unidimensional 
model of the BAOS-2 resulted in a good level of fit to the data, χ²(173) 
= 531.377, CFI = .951, TLI = .935, RMSEA = .052 (90% CI =.047; .057). 
As illustrated in Table 4, latent body acceptance by others was signifi­
cantly and positively related to body appreciation, compassionate self- 
responding (self-compassion), life satisfaction, healthy orthorexia, and 
self-esteem. On the other hand, body acceptance by others was signifi­
cantly and negatively related to orthorexia nervosa, anxiety, depression, 
and dieting.
4. Discussion
Overall, the results of the present study indicate that our novel 
French translation of the BAOS-2 retains its robust psychometric prop­
erties in a sample of French-Canadian adults. In terms of the factor 
structure of the French BAOS-2, the results of both EFA and CFA sup­
ported a unidimensional model with all 13 items retained. This model 
was fully invariant across both split-half subsamples. Yet, we also found 
that the BAOS-2 has differential item functioning based on respondent 
age, BMI and gender identity as function of subsamples. In both sub­
samples, higher respondent BMI was associated with lower body 
acceptance. Finally, we found strong evidence of convergent and con­
current validity for the French BAOS-2, with all associations with 
additional constructs in the expected direction.
In terms of factorial validity, EFA and parallel analysis supported the 
extraction of a unidimensional model of the French BAOS-2, with all 13 
items retained. This model was cross-validated using CFA in a second 
split-half subsample, with the model showing adequate fit indices and 
additionally achieving full invariance across split-half subsamples. This 
suggests that scores on the French BAOS-2 are best conceptualised as 
unidimensional, which is consistent with the results of both the parent 
study utilising an English variant (Swami, Todd, Stieger et al., 2021), as 
well as translations of the BAOS-2 in other linguistic groups (Behrend 
et al., 2023; Geller et al., 2024; Modrzejewska et al., 2024; Nerini et al., 
2024; Swami et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Importantly, where some 
previous studies have reported deflated item-factor loadings for Item 13 
(Modrzejewska et al., 2024; Swami et al., 2021), we found no evidence 
of this in the present work.
In a departure from the analytic strategies in previous studies, where 
examinations of measurement invariance have been limited to gender 
identity, we used hybrid MIMIC models to test for measurement biases 
as a joint function of age, and BMI, and gender identity. Our results 
indicated that all three variables were associated with item responses on 
the French BAOS-2, and inspection of paths indicated that across split- 
half subsamples some items intercepts were not invariant as function 
of age, and BMI, and gender identity. Relaxing the intercepts constraints 
of relevant items in a factors-only model allowed us to achieve a final 
model that was partially invariant across split-half subsamples. In this 
model, we found a lack of significant association between the BAOS-2 
latent factor and age and gender identity, respectively. Broadly 
speaking, these results are consistent with the finding that the BAOS-2 
achieves strong invariance across women and men, and that there are 
no meaningful gender differences in body acceptance by others 
(Behrend et al., 2023; Modrzejewska et al., 2024; Nerini et al., 2024; 
Swami et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Additionally, where associations 
between body acceptance by others and age have been investigated, the 
association has been found to be very weak (Wang et al., 2022).
On the other hand, our results indicated that participant BMI was 
inversely associated with body acceptance by others. Previous studies 
have generally reported that the association between body acceptance 
by others and BMI is inverse, although the strength of association varies 
across studies, from being negligible (e.g., Nerini et al., 2024) to 
weak-to-moderate (Moelle & Papps, 2025; Swami et al., 2021; Swami, 
Todd, Stieger et al., 2021) and others reporting no significant associa­
tion (Nerini et al., 2024). Swami, Todd, Stieger et al. (2021) have pre­
viously suggested that the negative association may reflect a cultural 
valuation of thinness that is increasingly found in most urbanised con­
texts (for a discussion, see Swami, 2015, 2021). Individuals who meet 
cultural prescriptions for thinness are, therefore, more likely to experi­
ence or perceive body acceptance by others. However, in keeping with 
recent discussions that have problematised the use of BMI (Bailey, 
Bessey, Lamarche, & Griffin, 2025), it is likely that BMI is only tapping a 
different underlying effect driven by weight bias internalisation. 
Consistent with this suggestion, Swami, Todd, Stieger et al. (2021) re­
ported that associations between body acceptance by others and weight 
bias internalisation were stronger than associations with BMI.
Finally, the results of the present study also suggest that the French 
BAOS-2 has adequate construct validity. More specifically, convergent 
validity was upheld through a positive and moderate-to-strong associ­
ation with body appreciation, while concurrent validity was supported 
through moderate associations with psychological well-being (anxiety, 
depression, life satisfaction, self-esteem), eating patterns (orthorexia, 
dieting behaviour), and self-compassion. Overall, these results are

consistent with previous findings indicating that greater body accep­
tance by others is associated with higher psychological well-being and 
lower symptoms of maladaptive eating (Avalos & Tylka, 2006; Swami, 
Todd, Stieger et al., 2021). However, the results also suggested that body 
acceptance by others was possibly more strongly associated with indices 
of well-being and self-compassion compared with negative eating 
patterns.
It may be that stronger associations with eating patterns would have 
been achieved had we included an index of adaptive eating. For 
instance, the Acceptance Model of Intuitive Eating (Avalos & Tylka, 
2006) suggests that greater body acceptance by others is associated with 
more positive body image, which in turn is associated with adaptive 
eating styles, such as intuitive eating. As such, we may expect stronger 
associations between body acceptance by others and an index such as 
intuitive eating compared with orthorexia or dieting behaviours. This 
would be a useful avenue for future research. Certainly, our results 
currently only hint at the value of the Acceptance Model in the 
French-Canadian context, but this could be formally tested as the 
necessary instrumentation – including the French BAOS-2 – are vali­
dated for French-speaking adults.
The present results should be considered in light of several limita­
tions of our work. First, because of recruitment method – via an online 
panel, supplemented by online community sampling – we caution that 
our sample of Qu´eb´ecois participants is unlikely to have been repre­
sentative. This sampling strategy may have introduced biases that, in 
turn, could have affected the generalisability of our findings. Addition­
ally, we did not gather potentially relevant information about urban­
icity, socioeconomic status and food insecurity, racialised identity, and 
religion or religiosity of our sample. In a similar vein, while the objective 
of the present study was to validate a French translation of the BAOS-2, 
it may also be interesting to examine invariance of the latent body 
acceptance by others constructs in a sample of Francophone and 
Anglophone Canadians. Relatedly, it may also be important in the future 
to examine the extent to which adaptations are necessary to ensure that 
the BAOS-2 can also be completed by younger age groups. This is 
important in view of findings showing that appearance-based com­
mentary is common in Qu´eb´ecois adolescents and have a negative 
impact on body image outcomes (Aim´e & Maïano, 2014; Gagn´e et al., 
2023). Finally, a small minority of participants reported having 
(currently or in the past) an eating disorder diagnosis or were pregnant 
at the time of study, and we do not know how these characteristics may 
have influenced our results.
Other limitations of the present study include the fact that we did not 
assess temporal stability or test-retest reliability of the French BAOS-2. 
These issues should be rectified in the future. Additionally, the assess­
ment of the construct validity of the French BAOS-2 could also be 
extended through the inclusion of measures of pressure to conform to 
appearance ideals. For instance, previous work has shown that body 
acceptance by others is negatively associated with pressure to conform 
to appearance ideals from the media, peers, family, and significant 
others (Nerini et al., 2024). Likewise, it will be important in future work 
to assess the degree to which the French BAOS-2 evidences discriminant 
validity (e.g., through associations with impression management).
Additionally, given that the data were cross-sectional, directional 
relationships could not be established. Although unconditional body 
acceptance by others is hypothesised to promote positive body image in 
the Acceptance Model (Avalos & Tylka, 2006), some researchers instead 
found that body appreciation predicted increased unconditional accep­
tance by others, but not the reverse, among German women and men 
(Behrend et al., 2023). Therefore, it may be that those with positive body 
image are more likely to seek out body accepting environments, as 
suggested by recent qualitative research (Alleva et al., 2023), than 
positive body image being shaped by them. Clearly, assessing longitu­
dinal relationships in future work within various geographic regions, 
including French-Canadian adults, is needed and would allow for tests of 
predictive validity and directionality (Wang et al., 2022).
Finally, our methodological choices may have affected data quality 
and validity. Given that the BAOS-2 is a self-report measure, we cannot 
rule out acquiescence and response biases, and given that the measures 
were administered in a pre-determined order without attention checks, 
we cannot rule out order effects and inattentive responding. In future 
work, it may also be useful to randomise the order of presentation of 
instruments within the survey to minimise order effects. Greater care 
could also have been taken to identify and screen out bots and those not 
paying full attention to the survey, although we note that these issues 
are typically minimised in panel responding.
Taken together, this study results indicate that our French translation 
of the BAOS-2 retains its strong psychometric properties, including a 
unidimensional factor structure and evidence of construct validity, in 
French-Canadian adults. We recommend two important next steps for 
researchers interested in the construct of body acceptance by others. For 
scholars in Quebec specifically, one important way to extend the current 
findings is to assess the fit of the Acceptance Model of Intuitive Eating – 
in which body acceptance by others is an important component – in 
Qu´eb´ecois populations. In a similar vein, the availability of the French 
BAOS-2 may be useful for practitioners with an interest in interventions 
that are focused on promoting body acceptance by others as a route 
toward more positive body image or healthier eating practices. For 
scholars internationally, it will be important to examine the extent to 
which latent body acceptance by others is invariant across national and 
linguistic contexts. This will help researchers better understand the ways 
in which cultural values and factors may shape body acceptance by 
others, which in turn has implications for the development and main­
tenance of positive body image.
